comment: Hand-authored Act-1 dialogue (takes precedence over the generated Drifella barks in content/drifella-barks.json). Two passes: (1) the opening-house cast (npcId->character is a first pass to refine during sprite work, e.g. Biscuit on the dog sprite); (2) a Morningside MILADY-LORE pass on reachable path NPCs (166/167/191/1410/163/136/202/138/146/1411/135/137) seeding the antagonist's lore through townsfolk rumor: Little Swag World is the organic source, Milady/Remilia corrupts it into hostile classification infrastructure (provenance gates, the leaked version, the Malady as visible symptom). See docs/swagbound-eb-act1-arc-mapping.md.

pages: A kid with a laminated smile steps into Bosch's lane before Bosch has finished choosing it.
"Hey. You want in? Membership's free. The read is what costs you. We already have a version of you that joined. You're just here to sign for it."
The kid points like an old injury learned to identify suspects.
"It was you. The record says you beat my buddy. It got here before you did."
"Beat it, before I have to become evidence."
He works the gum in his mouth like a clerk processing something wet and official.
"Spit, spit. You want some? Get your own, claimant. My mouth's the only file in town I still control."
"To tell you the truth. And the truth's been getting expensive. I don't remember saying half of what the town has me saying."
"My leaked version's a better neighbor than I am. Pays his dues, waves first. I just live here."
"Mifella's got a weapon with a reputation bigger than his hand."
"If you take it from him, maybe the town has to admit the source can still beat the brochure."
"You equip what you buy, or it's just a receipt that a better you knew the move."
"There. Handed my own enemy a hint. Confidence, or bad filing. We'll see which survives."
"Evening. Don't worry, you're the version I expected."
"What do you need filed?"
"In, in. Wipe whatever followed you off the mat."
"What can I do for you, the real one?"
Cash matters. I understand. But call your mother before the town does.
Worry's cheap. Calling back is the part nobody can copy yet.
The dog sighs like a manual nobody admits to reading.
Watch the shop window. Glow means upgrade, black means no fit.
Even a dog can tell when a system is ranking the bodies that walk in.
The machine waits the way machines wait. Patient, incurious, certain you'll comply.
"Insert your card. We'll take it from here. We always do."
The receiver's still warm. Somebody rehearsed your half of this.
Line's open. Say something before the record beats you to it.
She tilts her head at the door like it owes her money.
Who knocks at this hour? Be a dear and answer. I've decided it isn't for me.
That noise woke the whole house and you're treating fear as optional.
You'll sneak out regardless. Change clothes first, before the street files what you wore.
The dog cracks one eye from the civic sleep all decent animals keep.
People sleep. Dogs sleep. Rumors sleep. Why's your route still awake?
The kid grabs your sleeve like the sentence needed a hostage.
Took my brother out where the rock fell, then the cops bailed for the Sharks.
He's gone. My fault? No. Help me find him before my dad gets home? Yes No
The dog has filed a grievance with the sky and is waiting on a case number.
"(Fleas. Drinking me dry. I'm going to lie here and let them itemize me.)"
"I do what I can, which is mostly hold things and mean well."
"Take it. Roads make a person hungry, and hungry people sign the bad deals."
"Leave me whatever you don't want to carry. I keep it safer than secrets. Hold something? Yes No"
A voice arrives at the door before the face does.
"Somebody's knocking. That rhythm, this hour." A pause that files you. "A knock that wants something."
"Hey. The sound woke you too?"
"Was scared till I remembered: the town scares itself first, then asks us to confirm it happened."
"Feels like I'm always right here."
"It's not loyalty. The place keeps me in the same frame because the frame tests well."
"Welcome, how can I-" He stops. Recognition lands like a collector. "...Wait. You're the guy."
"After we threw down I got serious. Started showing up. Started training."
"Bit of pro wrasslin' now. Turns out losing once is just early-stage rebrand, baby."
Grill's on, patty's down, no cameras back here. What you eat off this counter never gets witnessed.
Every burger comes out the same. That's the point. Nothing here is one of one, and nobody's leaking your order.
"Hold on, kid. I sell hints because somebody monetized not-knowing before we got here."
"Come back when confidence costs more than cash. I'm always open. That's the whole model."
Hello. May I help you, or the other one of you?
I could warn you about the thing in the soup. But warnings work better if I bore you first.
Talk to everyone. The town tells a different story depending on when it thinks you're listening.
Cottage cheese on bread. The latest craze.
A food becomes a movement the second enough mouths agree they found it first.
Lucky sandwiches everywhere now. Fortune cookies, but the prophecy has mayonnaise.
Some give back what you spent. I don't know what you spent. I just know when a snack sounds like a prayer.
Sorry my kids gave you grief. They'll get theirs.
We loaned your dad a hundred grand. Or less. Nobody checks the number anymore.
Now we live in the poverty that loan bought. Aya - DUNG BEETLE. Hold still.
She watches her husband like a screen door in a hurricane.
Soft-hearted. Lets everyone in, signs whatever they hand him. Sweet, until you read what he let through.
He goes down harder than he planned. The surprise is the part that hurts.
I was thinner than my own legend. Weaker than the version of me that talks.
So it starts with you now. Lean close. I've got last words, and these ones are free.
"The kid next door chased a siren and told me to watch the house."
"So I'm watching it. Nothing's happened. That's the watching working."
"His folks are out. The kind of restaurant where the absence shows up on the bill."
"You're the one from next door. They tell me your family's underwater."
"Somebody around here loves itemizing a debt down to the penny."
"Keep plugging. The town forgives slow. But it does file the forgiveness."
"My dad got after me. No dessert. The rest of the decade."
"He said it the way a man reads a verdict he wrote himself."
"I've done my looking at that crater. Looked once. That was the quota."
"The town filed a feeling about it. I'd rather not refile."
"Some things you see once and spend the rest of your life unseeing."
"One night here and the whole party gets re-synced. Anyone who fainted, restored."
"Runs you $100. Sleep's the only clean transaction left."
"Morning, champ. Everyone's topped off. Door filed you out that way."
"Rate's steep. Clean sheets, locked door, a phone that tells the town you survived."
"People pay extra when the confusion's outside, counting heads."
The receiver clicks, the small sound of an institution recognizing a hand.
"Bosch gets to be a voice in another place before he's a body there."
Welcome to the front desk. Before I file you a room, the kiosk needs your card.
Insert your claimant card. No card, no record, and no record means no bed.
He clocks Bosch the way an actuary clocks a man on a ladder.
"You've got a face the Sharks like to itemize. Walk careful. The town keeps a version of you that already got jumped, and it'd hate to be proven right."
A man dabs his collar, filing a grievance no one will read.
"A pack of kids spat gum on me. On me."
"Now I'm evidence of something I never signed."
Your stay's on the record now. Checkout's whenever, but the log keeps the timestamp forever.
Sleep sound. Nobody leaks what happens between check-in and check-out. Not on this desk, anyway.
She sizes you up like an unscheduled audit.
"There's a process. There's a line."
"You're not even holding a number. Come back when you are."
He watches you with the patience of a camera already running.
"Seen you around. Lots, lately. Heh."
"Don't worry. I'm keeping notes for both of us."
"Town's confused. Confused crowds start auditioning for disaster."
"So we stay cautious. Caution's just surveillance that wears a badge."
"I'd rather be writing parking tickets. A meter doesn't argue about who it is."
"Lately every pedestrian arrives with a leaked version and a complaint from the future."
He looks too steamed to help, too official to ignore.
"Don't talk to me. I'm busy being angry in a way the file accepts."
He plants himself like a sign that grew a temper.
"So here you are. The one who wandered back down off the high road."
"DO NOT ENTER isn't a wall with words on it."
He stands at the angle of a man waiting for the worst to be official.
"Confusion's just a riot that hasn't finished the instructions yet."
"So we wait. Ultra cautious. To learn which version of today this is."
A gang kid leans in below the soundtrack.
"Don't file me with them. I'm a cop. The costume lies, I don't."
"Badge is real. Rest is wardrobe. Keep it off the record."
"Small fry. You're shaking. The form says suspects shake."
"A hero needs an origin. We just need a report."
"Your average cop beats any hero."
"A hero proves himself in public. We get issued the proof."
"Don't let the Mayor's praise swell your head."
"Civic praise is a leash with nicer words. Fight me before it tightens."
"Right here, right now. The script says that before the kid wins."
"If I lose, I call the boss. There's always another office behind this one."
"Let's go, go og. No, go. Even command slips when the record's nervous."
"Sorry. I meant to sound ready."
MiFella lets himself in like he owns the version of you that counts.
"Bosch! Wake up. You're trending before breakfast. Congrats, I think?"
"There's already a you outside. Cleaner haircut, smoother story."
"He signed a 4am mint: 'Swag is eternal.' The town's already calling him convenient."
"Relax, it's free press. Come let it count. ...You're gonna make a face, aren't you. They love the face."
He checks City Hall like a door he's sure he locked.
"I work for the place. The Mayor sits in my chest wrong. Smiles in the minutes, never in the room."
"Keep that off the record. You and me only."
The clerk reads aloud in the flat warmth of a form letter.
"No openings. No part-time. No nothing. 'At this time' so the no looks like it expires."
"It never expires. We just refile it under a newer date."
He brightens, then catches himself a beat too late.
"Saw something huge up on the high step once. Right where you're headed. And, oh."
"Forget I said it. They don't pay me to leak the route."
A man with a clipboard narrows his eyes toward the trees.
"Suspicious kids in the woods by the library. A gang's just a club the town forgot to stamp."
"So I started a movement. Sweeps the bad influences right out. Hum de dum dum."
"Foul mood. The Mayor doesn't care for me."
"This form could take hours to sign. Stalling a record is the only power this desk hands out."
"You're Bosch. Or a Bosch. Homework done? Teeth brushed? Bed dry?"
"Sorry. Grown-ups turn into checklists when they can't keep a kid off the route."
"Mayor Pirkle. You handled the toughs, which lets peace take the credit after."
"A key to the shack? Take it. But if the record turns violent, this office never signed off."
"Next vote, back Mayor Pirkle."
"A speech sounds sincere when someone else writes the need before you write the words."
"Thank you for your help."
"That's the line for the record. Off the record: thanks for making my file less embarrassing."
"City Hall sits east. Where the sun lands first and the paperwork warms up before anyone."
"Follow the light, if you trust light to tell you what it already touched."
"Trouble makes a market for hints. I'm a small vendor in a big market."
"I'll sell you a direction. I can't sell you the part where the direction sells you back."
"The old digger finally turned up something worth turning up."
"Says he'll only show it to one name. So the treasure already knows who to ask for."
"I wonder if EarthBound has been released yet."
"Sometimes a town waits for its own source material to arrive and explain why everyone sounds borrowed."
A mouse mother holds up her son like a small living exit clause.
"Take him if you get lost. Mazes underestimate anything they can't file as a person."
The mouse waits at the dark road's edge and asks for faith with no paperwork attached.
"Trust me here. Some routes only show up after the record stops watching."
"O o o o. Do I come too?"
"I'm small, I'm quiet, and I'm very hard for any system to explain."
I live rent-free with the Exit mice. They run faster than the paperwork.
When a maze claims you, it files the claim as a clerical error. Then keeps you.
Read a pamphlet: every hotel, every drugstore, a phone. Green costs a dollar. Black is free.
Convenience is surveillance with the instructions printed nice enough to feel like a gift.
"Lately I sense UFOs overhead. Maybe I'm dreaming."
"Dreams are cheaper to dismiss than witnesses, so the town files most witnesses under dreams."
The book says modern life demands a cash machine in every lobby. Demands it.
Took a whole book to make me notice the machines already knew when I was broke.
The person says nothing. In Morningside, silence still gets transcribed.
He won't talk. He's watching the version of you that showed up early.
He's decided this one waved better. .....
Cramming for the test. Hotels refill everything. A nap fixes the body, not the head.
A quiz about the rules is still the rules quizzing you. Memorize them first.
Studying the Step. North, then west of town. They say it carries a charge.
A place gets powerful when everyone points and nobody asks who collects.
A butterfly went past. Not in any ledger I keep. Just there, being light about it.
Stand near one and your cares unfile themselves. For a second nothing takes attendance.
Who're you visiting? Wheeled one in unconscious. Body's here, tenant's out.
Pay the fee, we hold the body on file till the person comes back to claim it.
Don't laugh. I'm a healer. Licensed by nobody, trusted by the desperate.
Hospital fixes the wounds with names. I take the ones too embarrassing to name.
Turned to stone, turned to mushroom. Conditions they can spell but won't forgive.
We sit in lobbies because the shame always checks in before the patient does.
I'll examine each of you. Small fee. Cheaper than a funeral.
The body turns into a line item, and the line item proves somebody's still in there.
Round here they say our doctor's number one.
That's what the lobby says. Lobbies make waiting sound like a choice.
Intake printer blinks: ROUTE FATIGUE ACCEPTED.
Clean breath. I can reset your meters. Not your story. Nobody resets that.
"A mouse put me here. A small mouse."
"The little ones hit hardest. The record never logs them, so they swing for free."
"Here for my daughter? You're the one who calls at night and talks for hours."
"Then why'd your version come down the line an hour before you came through the door?"
"Came to visit a friend. Slipped in the hall. Now I'm a patient too."
"That's the sad telling. The civic telling files it under efficient intake."
He says it flat, which is its own kind of dare.
"Doing this because I trust you. Basement first. We're not even kin."
"You've got me filed as a liar. Fair. But records get revised. Maybe I surprise us both."
"You've grown. Starting to look like a man."
"Or the town's idea of one. I prefer the rougher cut. The one still deciding."
"Dreamed you were traveling with a pretty girl."
"If the dream gets filed as record, tell her I said hi. Single folk flirt with futures."
"Take my cap. You've eyed it long enough."
"Wear something with a history. The route hates an object that remembers whose hands it passed through."
The kid shakes Bosch awake like an alarm clock he doesn't respect.
"You're up. Mifella's crew scattered, left me holding the dark. Typical."
"Home. Before they file us both as missing. Funny. I'm older, you're the one they copy."
He stands very still, like that counts as doing something.
"Heard a voice up the hill. Didn't check. I'm a busy man. When I work, I work clean."
The hill keeps the voice. He keeps the excuse. Everyone's holding something.
He guards the sign like the sign guards a secret. It does not.
"Board says Do Not Enter. You read, or do I read it to you?"
Go in anyway? Yes No
He's watched the hideout so long his eyes file their own reports.
"Lookout duty. Done my shift twice over. Take it, would you?"
Yes No
City Hall took the key the way a clerk takes a name. Quietly, for the file.
"Mifella's crew trashed the place, so it's sealed. Can't even change clothes."
He says Mifella like it's already on a list, and he's just reading it back.
"A library's honest work. Ignorance comes in, leaves with a due date."
"Got every Swag World fact in here. Maps too. Borrow free."
"Only loan in town that doesn't grow teeth."
Something surfaces from the dirt, having read your file and found it thin.
"Easy. Just an average mole. I have opinions about violence."
Want a few hints on fighting? Yes No
Past the gate the air starts itemizing you. Little ticks, like a register that can see.
That's the threshold. That's where Milady stops being a rumor and becomes a form you have to sign.
I walked up the line once to ask Milady a question.
The line asked me three back, kept the answers, and mailed me a cleaner version of the question. Never went again.
There's a point overlooking the sea, off thataway. Best view in the district.
Some horizons don't argue. They just take the file out of your hands.
Gets me in the throat every time. Don't log that I said so.
Everyone north of here works for Milady. Nobody north of here will say so.
That's the benefit package. You stop being able to say it, and in exchange you stop having to.
Everything here points up the line. Up the line is the yard, and the yard is Milady's.
And Milady's is everywhere, which is exactly how you know it's nowhere good.
Seen your leaked version yet? Smiles better than you. Answers before you do.
Milady prefers it, honestly. Less upkeep, no opinions of its own.
I'm not scared of Milady.
I'm scared of how reasonable she makes the paperwork sound. You sign before you notice you agreed.
They say Milady was born down in the source somewhere. They say she's already pregnant with the next one.
They say a lot. The saying sticks better than the truth. That stickiness IS the machine.
He smiles like a contract that signed itself and is waiting for you to notice.
This house? $7,500. Ocean view. The one address the town can't lock or copy.
A deed is the only version of you nobody can route around. Buy it?
Yes No
Hey. You've got your own place now. A real one, not a route.
Small fact that outlived a big town. Rarest kind there is.
He cradles the trumpet like the one possession the records haven't claimed yet.
"Happiest man alive. Scenic spot, good acoustics, nobody to tell me no."
"I'll send a haunting little melody clean across town. Let the leaked version of me try to play it back."
He squares up before you've decided anything, hungry to be your bad day.
You want to start something, or what?
He grins like a doorman working the rope outside a beating.
You want to meet Mifella? Course you do. You don't mind your name rearranged.
Real you or route you, the card clique files everybody the same.
Milady? Yeah, she's like, the brand. It's on everything down here. I stopped reading it.
Probably fine. It's usually fine. Why, did you read it?
My feed says Milady loves us. My feed also says I won a boat.
I did not win a boat. But I keep checking, you know? Just in case the boat's real and the love isn't.
That Malady up the route? That's only the part you're allowed to see.
Milady's the part already inside your distribution. You can't out-run your own reach.
The door's locked. The town is very good at that part.
Guardian checks the latch, then checks Bosch's pulse. Both, in that order.
Rest here. The house holds your place while the route tries to edit it.
A man leans uphill, already rehearsing the version of himself that arrived first.
"Something fell up there. First eyes own the story. I have owned nothing. I'd like a turn."
"Try and beat me. The copy of you already left."
He talks in fragments, like a confession the footnotes keep interrupting.
"Don't panic! It's just a... what... a rock that fell. I just want... who... to go home."
"The road's shut. They close it the way a clerk shuts a window: right as you reach it."
"We're going for the record anyway. The record doesn't care about the road. Neither do we."
She stares down the closed road like it filed paperwork against her personally.
"I want to go home. The road says no. One rock falls and the whole town acts processed."
The voice arrives before the face does, all curfew, no warmth.
"Don't you know what time it is? Get home. Before something logs that you didn't."
He recites his grievances in order, like reading items off a long receipt.
"A rock landed. The card gang's loose. You're sightseeing. And I'm hungry."
"I hate this job. Put that in the record too."
"The hilltop's dangerous. I'm advising against it, which is paperwork admitting it won't work."
"You'll go anyway. They always do. Sign here so the blame routes elsewhere."
A man surfaces from the dark, patient as a closing window.
"You. Curfew's a service we render. You're past your appointment."
The cop runs the same line on a loop, a stamp pressed until the ink gives out.
"Clear out. Get out of my way. Clear out. Get out of my way. Clear out. Of way out get my. "
"Whoops. My mistake. I mean: get out of my way."
"Shh. They're recording the quiet, and you're not in it yet."
She catches Bosch's eye like a witness who's relieved someone finally showed up to take a statement.
"Good timing. Will you do something about Pokey? He's a one-man membership drive and I'm exhausted."
"Are you two friends?"
Yes    No
The crater-light has him hooked, lips moving on a word with no end.
"That rock. It doesn't match anything on file. Marvelous. Unstamped. o o o o o!"
He waves you back, half rule, half mercy.
"Stop gawking. You're in the officer's frame. He's logging it, and you're not cleared to appear."
"Big footprint up the hill behind the shack, they say."
"Never saw it. The town agrees it's there without checking. A rumor with perfect attendance."
Remilia's the one holding the stamp.
Milady's the one who decided there'd BE a stamp. Different job. Same smirk.
Milady doesn't run Morningside. The town runs ON her, like a charge nobody itemized.
You'll meet her paperwork long before you meet her. Most people stop at the paperwork.
I got FREAKING FIRED FROM MILADY'S for asking what the job actually was.
Turned out the job was being me, at scale, badly. They had a cleaner me by lunch.
DO NOT ENTER, bolted where a welcome should be.
The font has won every argument it's ever had.
Used to be Little Swag World was just where we were from. No stamp, no fee.
Then Milady filed it as a 'source' and started charging us rent on ourselves. She calls that provenance.
The plaque swears this is the right house.
Bosch files it like anything from a man whose name is a confession spelled sideways.
Heat comes off the wreckage in slow waves, like it's deciding what to keep.
"Fire's still working. Burns here, then there. Like it's auditing what's left to take."
Don't stare at the cards too long. The cards read you back.
Whatever they find, they file straight up the line. Everything up the line is Milady's.
It stands in the one spot the map calls sacred, and grins like ownership is a thing you can grin into being.
"You finally got here. First Sanctuary on the list. Used to be yours, the brochure says. It's mine now. Come collect, if the source has the nerve."
"Twoson keeps beds by the night and records by the decade."
"Fifty dollars buys the bed. The receipt comes free because it already knows you."
Welcome. We log the order, we log the face. Mostly the order.
What'll it be?
You look hungry, or you look like the last guy who looked hungry.
Same difference. What do you need?
Step up. The counter doesn't bite. It files, but it doesn't bite.
Help you with something?
Two of you came in today. The other one didn't tip.
So. How can I help?
Welcome to the record. Want me to read you this place, or would you rather stay unfiled?
It's all here. Who got witnessed, who got the soft ban. Just say the word.
The old market feels unsafe. Wallets thin, claimants thinner.
But the fits down there are witnessed. Genuinely archive-grade. Worth the risk to your record.
I heard a claimant got scrubbed off the record. Deactivated against her will.
Kidnapped, they said. But you can't kidnap someone who was already deleted.
Some account was tailing the claimant. Following. Screenshotting.
No fit, no face you could file. It just wanted the read. Gave me chills.
Head east and you'll walk right into trouble.
Two deactivated places are waiting out there. Neither of them stays quiet for long.
Over in that Village, they've all converted to one feed. Fanatical about it.
Might be a fever. Might just be what happens when everyone follows the same source.
(There was a snatch. A whole self, dragged off the record.)
(Believe me. I'm deactivated, sure. Deleted, sure. But I still saw it. Still posting. Woof.)
Expand the mind, mon. The merchandise expands the wallet.
Come on. Buy some of my beautiful junk.
Hey. Why don't you buy the one true measuring stick? Rates any fit, any claimant.
Provenance guaranteed. Mostly. You in?
Fresh accounts! Never posted, never leaked. Nutritious and totally unwitnessed.
Please, take one before it gets flagged. Please?
The one who runs this soft-banned block is Everdred.
Ties to every leak and every disappeared handle around here. He knows who got filed.
I sell off the street, off the books, off the conscience.
The flies are seasoning. They volunteered.
Bananas? Good for the potassium. Bad for nothing else.
I move a lot of them. Don't ask where the peels go.
Looking for that so-called genius? The one everyone keeps witnessing?
As the real source around here, I'll tell you: he's out at the kiosks, looking for you.
We're rolling out to Dead Letter. The bus is loud enough that the deactivated can't get a read on us.
Want a seat? The ghosts won't file you if they can't hear you.
...re. Reactivating... no. Corrupted. My provenance won't load.
I was somebody's claimant once. Now I'm just the error where a name used to sit.
Sure, the fixer isn't a good person. He leaks people for a living.
But he's warm about it. Warmest doxx you'll ever get.
The old boss always watched the kiosks here. Kept the record honest.
That's why no crooked mod ever shook us down. He witnessed everything first.
I'm holding a spot in this queue. Waiting to get verified for today.
Might not make the list. They only witness so many of us before the ban resets.
That's right. We're the witnessed act. Everyone's got our fit, nobody's got our money.
And now they've got us. Held under a soft ban until we play their room.
We pull decent numbers, sure. But the claimant at the house by the preschool?
She's the whole town's read right now. Every feed points at her.
Just a hunch. The deleted ones in that tunnel hate anything upbeat.
Last time I passed through I was broadcasting something bright. They filed me hard for it.
I'm obsessed with one kid inventor. No, not the airheaded apple claimant.
The other one. Incredible. Fully witnessed. Never leaks his sources.
That mooch keeps asking me for something to eat.
He's the so-called source of new ideas. Let him invent his own dinner for once.
I'm a burner alt. My source, Apple Kid, has been waiting on you.
We nodded off holding the record open. Everything down here runs on tired.
Seasonings? Make every meal taste like it remembers a happier version of itself.
Miraculous. Nobody asks where the flavor's been filed.
Thinking of catching the bus to the soft-banned town.
They say deactivated things line the road. Wonder if the bus even gets filed as arriving.
Haven't I read your fit somewhere? At one of the witnessed shows?
So you're in the record too. Same scene, same soft ban.
That claimant isn't here. She deactivated without warning.
Just... unlisted herself and walked. No provenance, no forwarding. Gone from the record.
(Not an enemy. Just a friendly mole, off every list.
I'll teach you to read friend from foe, before the record does it for you.)
(The fever town is ahead. Everyone there posts the same thing.)
(Home of the apple claimant. Inventor. Fully witnessed.)
50 a night to be filed under one occupant. Rest here. Nobody reads you while you're down.
(Biggest kiosk row you'll ever get filed at. Every fit priced, every claimant witnessed.)
(The Deleted Five's tour bus. Still posting from a town that deactivated it.)
The statue glows wrong. A light with paperwork.
Bosch watches it the way you watch a door opening on its own.
"I've been waiting. Help me make the world blue. Simple. Easy to process."
"Be my right hand. A hand signs faster once the head stops asking who wrote the form."
"Aai i i i e e e e!"
"I screamed because nobody had issued me a better response yet."
"Blue, blue. Want to be one of us? Stop what you're doing and meet Mr. Carpainter."
"He loves a claimant who arrives pre-flattened."
"Blue, blue. Am I in your way?"
"I'll move. Even devotion yields to a route with a stronger appointment."
"Blue, blue. Don't bother us. We're praying."
"Fine. I'll move. The line to heaven is still a line."
"Blue, blue. You think I can only walk in place?"
"Watch. Even a loop can perform dissent if someone files the footage wrong."
"Green, green. No. That's last week's color. I haven't updated."
